content.text: Why Dashboard-as-Code Matters for GenAI Teams

Dashboards created through a UI accumulate invisible technical debt. Panels are edited in place, queries are tweaked without review, and within months the production dashboard bears no resemblance to anything in version control. When an incident occurs, no one is sure which panel definitions are authoritative or how to reproduce the dashboard in a new environment. For GenAI services — where dashboards cover token economics, quality signals, and provider-specific metrics that change with every model release — this entropy is particularly damaging.

Dashboard-as-code treats Grafana panel definitions, alert rules, and variable configurations the same way application code is treated: authored in text files, reviewed in pull requests, versioned in git, and deployed through a CD pipeline. The live dashboard is always a deterministic rendering of what is in the main branch. Manual edits through the UI are either blocked or automatically reverted.
The core invariant:

The state of your dashboards in production must match the state of the dashboard definitions in git. Any divergence — from a well-intentioned UI fix or an emergency panel tweak — is a configuration drift incident that should be detected and corrected within minutes.
Tooling Options
Three tools dominate the dashboard-as-code ecosystem for Grafana. They differ in abstraction level and composability.
Tool
Format
Best for
Trade-off
Grafonnet / Jsonnet
Jsonnet (.jsonnet)
Large teams needing a shared panel library with reusable components
Jsonnet learning curve; toolchain setup required
Terraform grafana provider
HCL (.tf)
Orgs already using Terraform for infrastructure; declarative apply/destroy
Terraform state management overhead; not human-friendly for complex panels
Grafana native JSON provisioning
JSON (.json)
Simple setups; direct Grafana API format; no extra dependency
Verbose; no composability; easy to produce unmaintainable files

Grafonnet with Jsonnet is the recommended choice for GenAI observability dashboards because the panel library pattern lets you define a standard GenAI panel once and reuse it across multiple dashboards — the token cost panel for the golden signals dashboard and the token cost panel for the cost attribution dashboard share the same source definition.
Repository Structure

A well-organised dashboard repository separates reusable library components from composed dashboards and alert rules. The library components enforce the metric naming and tagging standards defined in the telemetry conventions.

observability/
  dashboards/
    genai/
      golden-signals.jsonnet     # TTFT, error rate, token traffic, saturation
      token-cost.jsonnet         # cost attribution, spend rate, efficiency
      quality-drift.jsonnet      # eval scores, drift alerts, dimension breakdown
      provider-health.jsonnet    # per-provider latency, error rate, rate limits
    sre/
      on-call-overview.jsonnet   # all services: burn rate, incident queue
  alerts/
    genai-latency.yaml           # Prometheus/Alertmanager rules for latency SLOs
    genai-errors.yaml            # error rate and content_filter alerts
    genai-quality.yaml           # eval drift and quality SLO alerts
    genai-cost.yaml              # spend budget and anomaly alerts
  lib/
    genai-panels.libsonnet       # reusable GenAI-specific panel templates
    slo-panels.libsonnet         # burn rate gauges, error budget panels
    cost-panels.libsonnet        # spend rate, cost-per-token, forecast panels
    variables.libsonnet          # shared template variables (env, model, region)
  scripts/
    render.sh                    # jsonnet -> JSON for all dashboards
    validate.sh                  # lint + structural checks
    deploy.sh                    # push to Grafana API
    drift-check.sh               # compare git vs live Grafana state
Authoring with Grafonnet

Grafonnet is a Jsonnet library that provides typed constructors for Grafana panel types, datasource references, and dashboard metadata. The output is standard Grafana JSON that the API accepts directly.
Composing a Dashboard
CI Pipeline for Dashboard Changes

Every pull request that modifies a dashboard file triggers a CI pipeline that validates the Jsonnet syntax, renders the output JSON, and checks for structural correctness before any reviewer sees the PR.

# .github/workflows/dashboards-ci.yaml
name: Dashboard CI

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "observability/dashboards/**"
      - "observability/lib/**"
      - "observability/alerts/**"

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install Jsonnet and Grafonnet
        run: |
          go install github.com/google/go-jsonnet/cmd/jsonnet@latest
          go install github.com/jsonnet-bundler/jsonnet-bundler/cmd/jb@latest
          cd observability && jb install

      - name: Render all dashboards to JSON
        run: |
          cd observability
          for f in dashboards/**/*.jsonnet; do
            jsonnet -J vendor "$f" -o "rendered/$(basename "$f" .jsonnet).json"
          done

      - name: Validate dashboard JSON structure
        run: |
          cd observability
          python3 scripts/validate_dashboards.py rendered/

      - name: Lint alert YAML rules
        run: |
          promtool check rules observability/alerts/*.yaml

      - name: Post render preview link
        run: |
          echo "Dashboard diff: rendered JSON files are in the artifacts"
CD Pipeline: Deploying to Grafana

After merge to main, the CD pipeline applies the rendered JSON to Grafana via the HTTP API. The deploy script is idempotent: it uses the UID field to identify each dashboard and either creates or updates it.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# scripts/deploy.sh — deploy all rendered dashboards to Grafana
set -euo pipefail

GRAFANA_URL="${GRAFANA_URL:-http://grafana:3000}"
GRAFANA_API_KEY="${GRAFANA_API_KEY:?GRAFANA_API_KEY required}"
RENDERED_DIR="observability/rendered"

deploy_dashboard() {
  local json_file="$1"
  local title
  title=$(jq -r .title "$json_file")

  # Wrap in the grafana API envelope
  payload=$(jq '{dashboard: ., overwrite: true, folderId: 0}' "$json_file")

  response=$(curl -sS -X POST     -H "Authorization: Bearer ${GRAFANA_API_KEY}"     -H "Content-Type: application/json"     -d "$payload"     "${GRAFANA_URL}/api/dashboards/db")

  status=$(echo "$response" | jq -r .status)
  if [[ "$status" != "success" ]]; then
    echo "FAILED to deploy $title: $response"
    exit 1
  fi
  echo "Deployed: $title"
}

for f in "${RENDERED_DIR}"/*.json; do
  deploy_dashboard "$f"
done
echo "All dashboards deployed successfully."
Drift Detection

Manual UI edits to dashboards in production are the most common source of drift. A scheduled job fetches the live dashboard JSON from the Grafana API, compares it to the rendered JSON from the current git HEAD, and raises a PR alert or triggers an auto-revert if they diverge.
Template Variables for GenAI Dashboards

Template variables let a single dashboard JSON file serve all environments, models, and regions. For GenAI dashboards, the standard variable set includes environment, model, region, and provider. These must be defined consistently across all dashboards so users can switch context without re-setting variables.

// lib/variables.libsonnet
local g = import "github.com/grafana/grafonnet/gen/grafonnet-latest/main.libsonnet";

{
  datasource:
    g.dashboard.variable.datasource.new("datasource", "prometheus")
    + g.dashboard.variable.datasource.withRegex("Prometheus.*"),

  env:
    g.dashboard.variable.query.new("env", "label_values(genai_chat_request_total, env)")
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.withDatasourceFromVariable(self.datasource)
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.selectionOptions.withIncludeAll(false)
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.withCurrent("production"),

  model:
    g.dashboard.variable.query.new("model", "label_values(genai_chat_request_total{env="$env"}, model)")
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.withDatasourceFromVariable(self.datasource)
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.selectionOptions.withMulti(true)
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.selectionOptions.withIncludeAll(true),

  region:
    g.dashboard.variable.query.new("region", "label_values(genai_chat_request_total{env="$env"}, region)")
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.withDatasourceFromVariable(self.datasource)
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.selectionOptions.withMulti(true)
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.selectionOptions.withIncludeAll(true),

  provider:
    g.dashboard.variable.query.new("provider", "label_values(genai_chat_request_total{env="$env"}, provider)")
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.withDatasourceFromVariable(self.datasource)
    + g.dashboard.variable.query.selectionOptions.withIncludeAll(true),
}
Summary

Dashboard-as-code with a GitOps workflow eliminates the configuration drift and institutional knowledge loss that characterise manually managed observability. The workflow — author in Jsonnet, review in a PR, validate in CI, deploy via CD, and continuously check for drift — gives dashboards the same lifecycle guarantees as application code. For GenAI services, where dashboards covering token economics, quality signals, and provider metrics must evolve in sync with model releases and schema changes, this discipline is what makes the observability program maintainable at scale. The Grafonnet library pattern enables reusable panel components that enforce metric naming standards across every dashboard in the organisation.
